description: Speaker diarization via ONNX Runtime, auto device detection, and E2E validation

userStories.title: ONNX export script and GitHub Actions release workflow
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want a Python script that exports the pyannote segmentation model to ONNX and a CI workflow that builds and attaches the ONNX/PLDA artifacts to GitHub releases.
acceptanceCriteria: Python script at scripts/export_diarization_models.py that: (1) loads pyannote/speaker-diarization-3.1 segmentation model via pyannote.audio, (2) exports it to ONNX via torch.onnx.export with opset 17, (3) downloads the WeSpeaker ResNet34 ONNX embedding model from hbredin/wespeaker-voxceleb-resnet34-LM, (4) extracts PLDA .npz files (xvec_transform.npz, plda.npz) from the pipeline, (5) writes all artifacts to an output directory
Script validates the exported ONNX models by running onnxruntime inference on dummy inputs and comparing shapes
Script outputs: segmentation.onnx, embedding.onnx, plda_xvec_transform.npz, plda.npz

GitHub Actions workflow at .github/workflows/export-models.yml that: (1) triggers on release creation, (2) installs Python + pyannote.audio + onnxruntime + torch, (3) runs the export script, (4) uploads the 4 artifact files as release assets via gh CLI
README or doc comment in the workflow explaining how to trigger a model export manually
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: The segmentation model (PyanNet) is ~3-5MB as ONNX. The embedding model (WeSpeaker ResNet34) is ~85-95MB. PLDA files are ~2-4MB. Total ~100MB — too large to commit, hence the release asset approach. The export script needs pyannote.audio and torch installed. The WeSpeaker ONNX model may already exist on HF — check hbredin/wespeaker-voxceleb-resnet34-LM for a speaker-embedding.onnx file before re-exporting. For the segmentation model: input is (batch, 1, num_samples) float32 at 16kHz, output is (batch, num_frames, num_speakers) float32. Use a 10-second dummy input (160000 samples) for validation.

userStories.title: Diarization model download and caching
userStories.description: As a user, I want the diarization ONNX models downloaded automatically from GitHub releases and cached locally.
acceptanceCriteria: Add ort dependency to Cargo.toml (version 2, no default features, with ndarray feature)
Add ndarray dependency to Cargo.toml
New module src/diarize/mod.rs (or src/diarize.rs) with model download logic
Models cached at ~/.cache/fast-whisper-rs/diarization/ (respect XDG_CACHE_HOME if set)
Download function takes a GitHub release URL base (e.g. https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}/releases/download/{tag}/) and fetches: segmentation.onnx, embedding.onnx, plda_xvec_transform.npz, plda.npz
Skip download if files already exist in cache (check file exists, not content hash)
Show download progress with indicatif
Function to load ort::Session for segmentation and embedding models with execution provider selection (CUDA if available, else CPU)
Function to load PLDA .npz files into ndarray arrays
Unit tests for: cache path construction (with/without XDG_CACHE_HOME), file existence check logic
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Use reqwest (already a dep) for downloading. For .npz loading, the files contain numpy arrays — parse the .npz format (zip of .npy files) manually or use a crate like npyz. The PLDA files contain: xvec_transform.npz has 'mean' and 'transform' arrays, plda.npz has 'phi', 'sigma' arrays. ort execution providers: try CUDAExecutionProvider first, fall back to CPUExecutionProvider. Match the device logic from model.rs — if --device-id is a number, use CUDA; if 'mps', use CoreML (ort supports it); else CPU.

userStories.title: Sliding window segmentation inference
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the segmentation stage that runs PyanNet over sliding windows to produce frame-level speaker activity.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement sliding window over audio: 10-second chunks (160000 samples at 16kHz), step size = 10% of duration (16000 samples), yielding 90% overlap
For each window, run segmentation ONNX model via ort::Session producing (1, num_frames, num_speakers) output
Aggregate overlapping window outputs using overlap-add with Hamming windowing (matching pyannote's SlidingWindowFeature aggregation)
Apply binarization threshold (default 0.5) to produce binary speaker activity per frame
Handle edge cases: audio shorter than 10s (zero-pad), single window
Return struct with: binary speaker activity matrix (num_frames, num_speakers), frame duration, total audio duration
Unit tests for: sliding window index generation (verify overlap, coverage), Hamming window weights, binarization threshold logic
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: pyannote uses a 10-second window with segmentation_step=0.1 (so 1s step = 90% overlap). The aggregation uses overlap-add with a Hamming window to smoothly blend predictions from overlapping windows. The segmentation model outputs probabilities — these get binarized to 0/1 per speaker per frame. The frame rate is typically ~100 frames/second (determined by the model's internal stride). Warm-up: pyannote excludes 0.5s from each edge of a window during aggregation.

userStories.title: Speaker embedding extraction via ONNX
userStories.description: As a developer, I need to extract speaker embeddings for each detected speaker region using the WeSpeaker ONNX model.
acceptanceCriteria: Compute 80-bin fbank features from raw audio: 25ms frame length, 10ms frame shift, 16kHz sample rate (matching Kaldi/WeSpeaker convention)
For each (window, speaker) pair where speaker activity > 0, extract the audio segment and compute fbank features
Run WeSpeaker ONNX embedding model via ort::Session: input (batch, num_frames, 80) float32, output (batch, embedding_dim) float32
Batch embedding extraction for efficiency (configurable batch size)
Return embeddings as ndarray: (num_chunks, num_speakers, embedding_dim) with NaN for inactive speakers
Unit tests for: fbank feature computation (verify shape, known-input output), embedding output shape
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Fbank features: standard mel-frequency filterbank energies. Frame = 400 samples (25ms at 16kHz), hop = 160 samples (10ms). Apply pre-emphasis (0.97), Hamming window, FFT, then 80 mel-scale triangular filters, log energy. This matches torchaudio.compliance.kaldi.fbank. The WeSpeaker model expects fbank features, NOT raw waveform. Inactive speaker regions (no speech activity in that window) get NaN embeddings — these are filtered out before clustering.

userStories.title: Speaker clustering pipeline (AHC + PLDA)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need agglomerative hierarchical clustering with PLDA scoring to group speaker embeddings into distinct speakers.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement PLDA scoring: load LDA transform + PLDA model from .npz files, transform embeddings into PLDA space (LDA dimension reduction to 128), compute pairwise PLDA log-likelihood ratios
Implement agglomerative hierarchical clustering (AHC): cosine distance on L2-normalized embeddings, average linkage, threshold-based stopping criterion (default threshold 0.6)
Support --num-speakers (exact count), --min-speakers, --max-speakers constraints on clustering
Filter out NaN embeddings and inactive speakers (< 20% activity in their window) before clustering
Return hard cluster assignments: mapping from (chunk_index, speaker_index) -> global_speaker_id
Unit tests for: L2 normalization, cosine distance, AHC with known embeddings (verify cluster count), PLDA transform shapes, speaker count constraints
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: pyannote's default clustering is VBx (AHC + Variational Bayes refinement with PLDA). For a first pass, AHC with PLDA scoring is sufficient — VBx refinement can be added later. PLDA transform: center embeddings (subtract mean), apply LDA matrix, then compute log-likelihood ratios between pairs. AHC: start with each embedding as its own cluster, merge closest pair until threshold is reached or speaker count constraints are satisfied. Use ndarray for all linear algebra. The threshold hyperparameter (0.6 default) controls over/under-clustering.

userStories.title: Diarization post-processing and CLI integration
userStories.description: As a user, I want speaker labels assigned to transcript chunks when diarization is enabled.
acceptanceCriteria: Reconstruct speaker timeline from cluster assignments: map (chunk, speaker) cluster IDs back to continuous time segments with speaker labels (SPEAKER_00, SPEAKER_01, ...)
Merge consecutive segments from same speaker (matching diarize.py lines 81-112 exactly)

Align diarization segments with ASR transcript chunks using argmin on end timestamps (matching diarize.py lines 115-152 exactly): for each diarizer segment, find closest ASR chunk end timestamp, assign that speaker to those chunks
Update output.rs: when diarization is active, speakers field contains [{speaker, text, timestamp}, ...] per-chunk with speaker labels (matching Python's non-grouped output format)
Wire into main.rs: diarization runs when --hf-token is not 'no_token' (matching Python: if args.hf_token != 'no_token')
Print diarization success message variant: 'Voila!✨ Your file has been transcribed & speaker segmented go check it out over here 👉 {path}'
Integration test: given known diarization segments and known transcript chunks, verify speaker assignment matches Python behavior
Unit tests for: segment merging (consecutive same speaker, alternating speakers, single segment), argmin alignment (exact match, closest match, null end timestamps)
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: The Python post-processing is in insanely-fast-whisper/src/insanely_fast_whisper/utils/diarize.py. Key details: (1) segment merging uses the START of the next different-speaker segment as the END of the current super-segment, (2) argmin alignment progressively crops the transcript list for efficiency, (3) null end timestamps in ASR chunks are treated as f64::MAX for argmin, (4) group_by_speaker is always false in insanely-fast-whisper (each ASR chunk gets a speaker, not grouped). The speakers field in output JSON changes from [] to the list of {speaker, text, timestamp} dicts.

userStories.title: Auto-detect Metal on macOS
userStories.description: As a macOS user, I want the CLI to automatically use Metal GPU when available without requiring --device-id mps.
acceptanceCriteria: When compiled with 'metal' feature and running on macOS, auto-select Metal device when --device-id is the default '0'
Explicit --device-id mps still works as before
Explicit --device-id <number> on macOS skips Metal auto-detection (for CPU fallback with '0' on non-Metal Macs, this is handled by Metal init failure -> CPU fallback)
Print info message to stderr when auto-selecting Metal: 'Using Metal GPU (auto-detected)'
Unit test for auto-detection logic (compile-time cfg test)
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Use #[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", feature = "metal"))] to gate the auto-detection. The change is in src/model.rs select_device(). When device_id == '0' and we're on macOS with metal feature, try Device::new_metal(0) first. If it fails, fall back to CPU as before. This is a small quality-of-life improvement.

userStories.title: End-to-end validation and diarization integration tests
userStories.description: As a developer, I want integration tests that validate the full diarization pipeline and E2E output parity with the Python version.
acceptanceCriteria: Integration test (tests/diarization_integration.rs) for full diarization pipeline: segmentation -> embedding -> clustering -> post-processing, using mock ONNX sessions or small test models
Integration test validating diarization is triggered only when --hf-token is not 'no_token'
Integration test for model download/caching: verify cache directory structure, skip-if-exists behavior
Unit test for output JSON with diarization: verify speakers field contains [{speaker, text, timestamp}, ...] entries with correct speaker labels
All existing tests continue to pass (no regressions)
cargo check passes
cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Tests that require actual ONNX model files or GPU should use #[ignore]. Tests for the post-processing logic (segment merging, argmin alignment) should work without models — use synthetic data. For the mock ONNX approach: either use small hand-crafted ONNX models (single linear layer) or test the pipeline stages independently with known inputs/outputs.
